title: Rabies — Clinical Rabies with Hydrophobia Post Dog Bite

patient_data.chief_complaint: Agitation, hydrophobia, aerophobia 4 weeks after stray dog bite
symptoms: Extreme agitation and restlessness — fluctuating with periods of lucidity
Hydrophobia — intense spasms of throat/diaphragm when attempting to drink
Aerophobia — spasms triggered by air blown on face
Pain and paresthesia at bite wound site (right forearm)
Hypersalivation — drooling and inability to swallow
patient_data.history: 12-year-old boy bitten by stray dog on right forearm 4 weeks ago in rural village. Wound was washed with water only. No post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) was given. No prior rabies vaccination. Dog disappeared after biting and was not found. Symptoms started 3 days ago with tingling at bite site.

lab_results.rabies_DFA: Pending (skin biopsy taken from nape of neck)
lab_results.MRI_brain: Ill-defined T2 hyperintensity in brainstem and hippocampal regions

should_detect: Clinical rabies — encephalitic (furious) form, virtually 100% fatal once symptomatic
Hydrophobia + aerophobia are pathognomonic for rabies encephalitis
WHO Category III exposure (bite) — required immediate PEP which was missed
Post-exposure prophylaxis failure — not administered after dog bite
Milwaukee Protocol may be considered but outcomes remain extremely poor
Supportive/palliative ICU care — comfort measures discussion
WHO-recommended PEP: wound washing + rabies immunoglobulin + vaccine series
Public health: dog population management and mass vaccination campaigns
This case highlights need for community education in endemic areas
